summary: DHH reflects on how his early perception of the internet—that small companies should fake being big—was completely wrong. He argues the real advantage of being a small company online is leaning into smallness: being personal, human, and even offensive while still reaching a global market. He uses TextDrive's photo stream of their office move as an example of mundane business moments creating genuine connection. The post captures an early articulation of the authenticity-over-polish philosophy that would define 37signals.
key_points: The early internet instinct was to use anonymity to appear bigger than you are, but that impulse was misguided
Small companies' real competitive advantage online is authenticity—being personal, human, and unfiltered
Mundane behind-the-scenes content creates genuine relationships with customers
Smallness scales globally on the internet without requiring corporate pretense
money_quotes: The cool thing about working on the net as a small company is exactly that you can act even more like one.
You can be personal, human, interesting, offensive, and still manage to scale to a world-wide market.
It's a wonderful window into what might be mundane every-day business, but its making me care and be interested because of the relationship.
key_insight: Small companies shouldn't fake being big—their real internet advantage is leaning into authenticity and human connection at global scale.
